items.title: 6 underrated Ryobi tools that will save you hours in the workshop

content.content: Every workshop has its headliners — the circular saw that rips through plywood, the drill that lives on the pegboard, the sander that gets pulled out for every furniture project. I've been adding to my Ryobi ONE+ collection for years now, and somewhere along the way I noticed something. The tools I grab most often aren't the big names. They're the ones that handle the annoying stuff, the cleanup, the tasks that used to eat up half my afternoon before I even started the real work. You won't find these six on most "must-have" lists, which honestly surprises me. They share one 18V battery platform, and together they've probably saved me more hours than my table saw.

items.author: Jonathon Jachura

items.title: I don't click "Accept Cookies" anymore thanks to this extension

content.content: Cookie pop-ups suck. I don't know a single person who doesn't think the same, and they get in the way all the time. They waste time, can cause websites to break if you don't answer correctly, and I've even encountered a few "pay to remove the cookies" notifications.

items.author: Gavin Phillips

items.title: The smartest sci-fi movie on Netflix isn’t the one you think

content.content: There are a lot of factors to consider when looking for a new sci-fi movie to watch. Do you want to watch something recent, or are you down to sample some 20-year-old classics? Do you want something short you can watch without fear it'll keep you up too late, or something so absorbing you won't care that it'll absorb you long past your bedtime? Do you want something scientifically accurate or more of a space opera that fudges the rules of psychics for the sake of entertainment?

items.author: Dan Selcke

items.title: You’re probably underusing your phone’s USB-C port — I use it for these 5 things

content.content: Most people never use their phone’s USB-C port for anything other than charging the device. At best, they might plug in a USB flash drive to move a few files and call it a day. But that small port at the bottom can handle far more than most of us give it credit for.

items.author: Pankil Shah

items.title: 5 ways to make "dumb" appliances smart for under $20

content.content: Smart home electronics feel like a thing of the future, even though they're very much available now. I've always been curious about how this technology can be helpful to others, or even to me, and I've spent a fair amount of time and money making that happen over the years. But since my brother got hurt in a car accident roughly 10 years ago, I've been searching for ways to make his life easier with smart home tech.

items.author: Shaun Cichacki

items.title: That USB port on your power strip isn’t as safe as it looks

content.content: Power strips, and more specifically surge protectors, are essential technology products that everyone should own. They shift the extra electrical currents to your outlet's ground wire in the event of a power surge, protecting your devices or appliances from costly damage. This functionality cannot be understated, as it could reduce the likelihood of an electrical fire if there are unstable power fluctuations, major surges, or overheating. From an everyday convenience perspective, though, power strips are practical because they expand the number of outlets you can access.

items.author: Brady Snyder

items.title: 6 winter emergencies you can solve with a cordless heat gun

content.content: My furnace quit during an ice storm two winters ago. The wind chill was somewhere around -40°F, the house was dropping fast, and I had no clue what was wrong. The thermostat looked fine, the filter was clean, and the breaker hadn't tripped. I finally trudged outside and discovered ice had completely sealed over the exhaust pipe screen — the furnace couldn't vent, so it shut itself down. Five minutes with my Ryobi ONE+ 18V Cordless Heat Gun and everything was running again. That $84 tool has helped me out with at least six other winter messes since then, and now I keep it handy from November through March.

items.author: Jonathon Jachura

items.title: Life without the 3.5 mm jack is still worse

content.content: We've been sold a narrative for nearly a decade now: the removal of the 3.5mm headphone jack from smartphones was necessary progress. Thinner phones, better water resistance, the future is wireless, we were told.

items.author: Yadullah Abidi

items.title: This $23 moisture meter can save you thousands in water damage

content.content: Water damage is one of those things that doesn't cross your mind until you're holding a contractor's quote and feeling slightly nauseous. Moisture has a way of hiding where you won't find it. From drywall and subfloors to wall cavities — it settles into the parts of your house you never look at and wouldn't know how to access anyway. A stain on the ceiling or some weird smell you can't place might be the first hint that something's wrong.

items.author: Jonathon Jachura

items.title: 4 settings to make your Pixel even smarter that you probably forgot to change

content.content: The latest Google Pixels, alongside the last several generations of the device, have so many features and tools that make the Pixel line smarter than other phones. I've spent time digging through menus and have found several settings you can change to make your Pixel even smarter.

items.author: Brandon Miniman

items.title: I stopped buying smartwatches for these simple reasons

content.content: Being a technology geek, I am a person who loves getting the latest piece of tech or gadget as soon as it launches. I remember the time when smartwatches broke into the scene and how everyone, including me, was quite fascinated with them. Samsung Gear, launched in 2013, and the Apple Watch, launched in 2015, were (and are now as well) the crème de la crème of smartwatches.

items.author: Sagar Naresh

items.title: These 4 bathroom upgrades make a bigger difference than any renovation

content.content: Cleanliness is one of the most important facets when it comes to a home. If you're living in filth, you're more likely to get sick, have poor hygiene, and no one will ever want to come to your house. One of the most important places to keep clean in your home is the bathroom.

items.author: Chris Hachey

items.title: The streaming service I use the most isn’t Netflix or Prime Video

content.content: For a long time now, different studios have been launching their own streaming services, hoping to knock Netflix off its place at the top of the pile. Several years into the so-called streaming wars, it's pretty clear that won't happen; Netflix remains the dominant player in the space, with everyone else fighting for second place.

items.author: Dan Selcke

items.title: I removed these Windows startup apps and my PC finally felt fast

content.content: The faster your PC boots, the sooner you can start doing what you want with it. If you’ve never configured your computer’s startup apps, then you’re wasting time every time you turn it on.

items.author: Ben Stegner

items.title: 10 things you should try on your Amazon Kindle besides reading books

content.content: After years of using a Kindle Paperwhite and the Kindle app on iOS and Android, I’ve found that the device’s distraction-free nature is actually its greatest strength for tasks that have nothing to do with traditional reading.

items.author: Saikat Basu

items.title: 3 common USB-C mistakes that ruin your charging speed

content.content: When it comes to maximizing the charging speed capabilities of your device, whether it be a phone, tablet, computer, or other gear, there are a few things that need to fall in line. Just because a device can charge at a specific wattage doesn't mean it will. You need the right cable, adapter, and outlet to facilitate this. Yes, there are differences.

items.author: Christine Persaud

items.title: 5 Android subscriptions you can easily replace with free apps

content.content: I pay monthly subscriptions for a bunch of apps. Some, like Adobe's Lightroom mobile, are genuinely worth the cost for how effortless it makes photo enhancements. None of the alternatives even come close to its combination of intuitiveness, speed, and sophistication.

items.author: Tashreef Shareef

items.title: I used AI to plan my week and it actually saved time

content.content: 2026 has officially begun, and if you’re still avoiding AI in your workflow, it’s frankly time to stop fighting it. The more you do, the quicker you’ll get left behind. I understood this when OpenAI released ChatGPT to the public in 2022, and ever since, I’ve been hunting for new ways to fold AI into my routine.

items.author: Mahnoor Faisal

items.title: This alternative to Chrome is unbelievably fast

content.content: Chrome is typically my go-to browser, and I spend most of my time in Google's four walls. For me, Chrome works fine, and while its privacy credentials aren't great, it consistently ranks as the most secure browser you can use.

items.author: Gavin Phillips

items.title: Most sci-fi shows/movies get space wrong, but this series actually does the math

content.content: There's an expectation that science fiction stories at least try to adhere to the rules of real-world science; after all, the word is right there in the name of the genre. But every sci-fi fan learns to let some things slide. Star Trek and Star Wars are both science fiction, but they both have methods of travel — the Warp Drive and the Hyperdrive — that allow characters to zip through the vast emptiness of space at speeds inconceivable by anyone in our world. In an old episode of Star Trek: The Original Series, the Enterprise is attacked with a sonic weapon despite the fact that sound cannot exist within the vacuum of space.

items.author: Dan Selcke

items.title: 3 free macOS apps that fix problems Apple still hasn’t

content.content: macOS has a lot going for it: seamless integration with other Apple devices, fantastic security, and stellar performance. That's especially the case with the energy-efficient and powerful Apple Silicon, leading people to use older MacBooks longer than ever before.

items.author: Brandon Miniman

items.title: 4 low-tech gadgets that solve high-tech problems in your home

content.content: Upgrading your home doesn't mean that you need to buy the latest and greatest device on the market. While it may be cool to splurge on a fancy new purchase, like a new TV, most of your purchases for your home aren't going to be as sexy. I am always thinking about the reality as a homeowner of buying something and thinking about how a large chunk of money just went out the window for one of the least exciting purchases you've ever made, like a hot water heater or a fence.

items.author: Chris Hachey

items.title: Cable standards are a mess, but you shouldn’t blame USB-C

content.content: USB-C, officially known as USB Type-C, was billed as the port to replace them all. It's a universal, reversible connector that replaces the likes of USB-A, USB-B, Lightning, and Mini DisplayPort. In fact, all other USB connectors were designated as legacy in 2014. While you'll still see USB-A, USB-B, and their derivatives out in the wild, it's clear that USB-C is the future. In theory, the USB-C connector gave us everything we wanted — we can now charge and transmit data from headphones, smartphones, laptops, and more with the same cable. So why aren't we happy?

items.author: Brady Snyder

items.title: NotebookLM is great, but it shines when you use it like this

content.content: There aren’t a lot of productivity tools I use every single day beyond the basics: Google Calendar, Google Tasks, Notion, and the usual Asana/Trello setup for work. But one AI product that has actually stuck around for me is Google’s NotebookLM. Its premium tier is included with my Google plan, and it's packed with all the right features I look for in a tool.

items.author: Mahnoor Faisal

items.title: I can’t browse on Android without these 5 extensions

content.content: I love browsing on my phone, but I hate everything that comes with it. Intrusive ads, broken dark modes, and videos that stop the moment you switch apps. One of the things that makes browsing on a PC fun is the third-party extensions. I rely on a few of them, and they fix some of the worst parts of the internet.

items.author: Pankil Shah

items.title: Gemini's newest feature is going to make it a whole lot more usable

content.content: Google's Gemini is probably my most-used AI assistant, but there's no denying it sometimes takes a minute to get an answer to what seems like a simple question. A new update from Google aims to help reduce this friction by letting you skip "in-depth" thinking on certain models.

items.author: Dave Schafer

items.title: 5 real world examples where Excel's MAP function shines

content.content: If you're using Microsoft Excel 365, 2021, or 2024, then you have access to dynamic array functions. These functions take productivity to another level thanks to their "spill" functionality. This means a single dynamic array function returns multiple results that spill into adjacent cells, simplifying complex array operations. One that Excel pros use widely is the MAP function.

items.author: Chifundo Kasiya

items.title: I'm replacing my $1,200 phone with a $200 one for this feature alone

content.content: Many of us struggle with forming healthy relationships with technology and social media, myself included. Too much screen time is rarely a good thing, but setting realistic boundaries is difficult. Smartphones and mobile apps are designed to suck you in and hold your attention, filling your screen with vibrant colors, endless feeds of content, and an inevitable dopamine rush. In my quest to use my Android phone intentionally, I wondered what it'd be like to use a phone that doesn't always seek my immersion.

items.author: Brady Snyder

items.title: You’re probably backing up your data the wrong way

content.content: Cloud backup tools have made us lazy about data security. We just set it on Auto and forget that it exists. We confuse synchronization (instant updates) with backup (historical preservation). While the cloud is seamless, it lacks the true ownership of a local server—even if setting one up feels archaic and complicated.

items.author: Keval Shukla

items.title: How I set up the perfect guest network for my smart home devices

content.content: Building a secure and efficient smart home takes more than just a fast internet connection. You also need to consider network segmentation, depending on your network's size. Many homeowners achieve this by setting up a guest network to separate smart devices from personal ones, enhancing both security and performance. One of the easiest systems for this setup is also among the most popular Mesh Wi-Fi networks for your home.

items.author: Bryan M. Wolfe

items.title: 3 Netflix shows to watch after Stranger Things

content.content: The fifth and final season of Stranger Things landed on Netflix in late 2025, capping off an epic story nearly 10 years in the making. In an era where TV shows are spread out over a variety of streaming services, and it's hard for any one series to become hugely popular, Stranger Things became a cultural phenomenon, a show you were either watching or had heard of. Now that it's gone, there's a hole in our schedules. What can possibly replace it?

items.author: Dan Selcke

items.title: I’ve used Samsung for years, but these 5 features make me feel like a pro

content.content: If there’s one thing Samsung does really well than any other smartphone company, it’s giving you way more than you expect. A lot of them are quite popular, like Good Lock, Edge Panel, Samsung Dex, and Galaxy AI. But there are also plenty that fly under the radar because they’re hidden or not talked about enough.

items.author: Pankil Shah

items.title: I stopped using CCleaner and Windows tools did the same job

content.content: Years ago, CCleaner was a regular part of my Windows setup. Whenever I bought or built a new PC, it was one of the first utilities I installed. I used it mainly to clear temporary files and browser data from a single place, instead of digging through Windows menus.

items.author: Digvijay Kumar

items.title: Why I uninstalled almost everything on my laptop (and what I use instead)

content.content: Here is the thing about modern native apps — they are stubborn. Even when disabled at startup, many leave background "helper" processes running to check for updates or sync data, which lurk in the system tray and eat up resources. I kept these apps installed because I was led to believe that installing them was the best way to get the most out of Windows.

items.author: Keval Shukla

items.title: 5 signs that mean your PC parts are failing

content.content: Computers don't require a lot of maintenance. The occasional dusting and cleaning out the fans is more than enough to keep them running for just about as long as you want. However, the parts inside your PC do have a lifespan, and hardware failures rarely announce themselves with a polite warning.

items.author: Yadullah Abidi

items.title: I didn’t expect this Samsung feature to be a privacy nightmare

content.content: When you set up a Samsung account on your Galaxy phone, you agree to a long list of terms and permissions that you probably don’t read. And honestly, I don’t blame you. The excitement of setting up a new phone makes it easy to miss the fine print. But among those default options is something called Customization Service.

items.author: Pankil Shah

items.title: This tiny notes app made me write more because it opens instantly

content.content: Most of our ideas don’t arrive fully formed, well-polished, or at the best time. You can sit at your desk staring at a blank page, and the moment you stand up to grab a sip of juice from the fridge, the headline pops up in your mind. Those are the moments when you reach for the nearest piece of paper and scribble the idea down as quickly as possible, hoping it doesn’t disappear before you make it back to your chair.

items.author: Adaeze Uche

items.title: You’re probably wasting battery life with this common habit

content.content: You're accidentally frying your laptop battery. If it spends its life plugged in at your desk, cranked up to 100 percent charge at all times, you're degrading the battery faster than you realize.

items.author: Gavin Phillips

items.title: Your smart TV is tracking more than you realize

content.content: Every day we move seamlessly between smartphones, laptops, smartwatches, and smart TVs, often without giving a second thought to what happens in the background. With so many connected devices in constant use, our personal data is shared more than we realize. And that's something I'm certainly not okay with. For me, privacy and control over my personal data matter just as much as the features these devices offer.

items.author: Shimul Sood

items.title: I stopped using this popular Android feature and my phone runs better

content.content: I used to think that it's normal for any Android smartphone to slow down over time. As updates get heavier and I install more apps on my phone, performance eventually takes a hit. Later on, I realized that that’s not entirely true. Out of curiosity, I started experimenting with different settings on my phone. While doing all of this, I discovered that turning off an extremely popular Android feature, which most of us leave on by default, made my phone run a lot faster. App launches felt snappier, scrolling was smoother, and the battery lasted longer.

items.author: Kanika Gogia

items.title: I stopped losing heat through my windows with this cost-effective DIY kit

content.content: My three-season room is maybe 15 feet from the living room. A glass slider separates them. There are no ducts running out there, no vents, and nothing tying it to our HVAC system (besides keeping the slider open) — so it roasts in summer and turns into a freezer come January.

items.author: Jonathon Jachura

items.title: 5 Insta360 settings you should change immediately

content.content: 360-degree cameras are incredible, but the promise of capturing everything can quickly get buried under layers of confusing settings. Your shiny new Insta360 might advertise 8K 30fps recording out of the box, but that spec alone doesn’t guarantee great footage—or even usable files.

items.author: Yadullah Abidi

items.title: This one NotebookLM habit changed how I reread difficult texts

content.content: Reading difficult texts is getting harder because of dips in attention spans for some of us. We mistake approaching long-form essays, nuanced reporting, and research-heavy articles with the same habits we use for news feeds and newsletters. We skim and move on. Often not realizing that familiarity isn't understanding. My first attempt was to take better notes. My second attempt is using NotebookLM as a slow reading companion. I already use NotebookLM for reading a hoard of bookmarked articles. So, it's a natural experiment to use it for longform articles without sacrificing focus and momentum.

items.author: Saikat Basu

items.title: These highly-rated apps are leaking your data — find out if you're affected

content.content: Security firm CovertLabs has found that nearly 200 apps on the Apple App Store are leaking user data for millions of users. In a post on X, CovertLabs described the situation as "as bad as it gets."

items.author: Dave Schafer

items.title: 5 of my favorite Pixel exclusives you can use on older and budget phones

content.content: Google is known for being generous with new features, bringing them to as many smartphones as possible. To that end, we've seen flagship tools like Gemini Live and Circle to Search not only debut on Google Pixel phones, but also come to Android phones at large. Those features in particular are even available on iOS via the Gemini and Google apps. However, some of Google's most exciting Pixel exclusives of late have been restricted to flagship Pixel 9 and Pixel 10 series models due to hardware constraints.

items.author: Brady Snyder

items.title: 4 Gemini features you're not using on your Android phone (but should be)

content.content: For now, most people's integration with Gemini happens on a phone, and Android has the deepest integrations and most tools to help you in your daily life. A lot of these features that offer a lower-friction way to interface with Gemini, you might not be using. Let's explore a handful of Gemini features that you're probably not using on your Android phone, but should be.

items.author: Brandon Miniman

items.title: I edited the Windows Registry without fear using this safer editor

content.content: There are few places in Windows as terrifying as the Registry Editor. One misplaced keystroke, one accidentally deleted value, and you could be staring at a boot loop or scrambling for how to use System Restore on Windows. The built-in Registry Editor (regedit) offers no safety net, no second chances, and certainly no forgiveness for mistakes. It is precise, but it does nothing for your peace of mind.

items.author: Oluwademilade Afolabi

items.title: I switched to a USB-C hub with a touchscreen and it's a game-changer for device monitoring

content.content: Chances are, you probably own or use a USB-C hub, dongle, or adapter to connect to your favorite devices and accessories. The switch to USB-C created one connector to rule them all, but it also marked the beginning of dongle life for many. If you use USB-A accessories, take photos or videos, or frequently hook up to external monitors, your laptop or tablet's onboard ports likely won't cut it. That's why USB-C hubs and docks are a dime a dozen these days, but what if one offered something special?

items.author: Brady Snyder

items.title: This $35 gadget finally fixed the musty smell in my bathroom

content.content: It's hard to argue that bathrooms are the worst-smelling rooms in your home. It doesn't take a rocket science degree to figure out why, and we're adults here, so we don't need to go into the reason any further. But just because everyone has to deal with the same "stuff" in the bathroom doesn't mean all bathrooms have to smell bad.

items.author: Chris Hachey

items.title: I turned off these 5 smart TV settings and it stopped spying on me

content.content: You probably don't realize it yet, but the sleek smart TV sitting in your living room has become a surveillance device masquerading as entertainment. And the worst part? Most of us unknowingly enabled it during setup.

items.author: Yadullah Abidi
